explicit_text: I was born in Thailand in 1989, in a refugee camp where my family lived after migrating from Laos. We stayed there because of the Vietnam War, but the Thai government no longer wanted the Hmong people to remain. Living in that camp was difficult, and it was the starting point before we immigrated to the United States in 2004.
implicit_text: I was born in a Southeast Asian country in 1989, in a refugee camp where my family lived after migrating from a neighboring landlocked nation. We stayed there because of the conflict in Vietnam, but the government of that country no longer wanted the Hmong people to remain. Living in that camp was difficult, and it was the starting point before we immigrated to the United States in 2004.

explicit_text: My parents originally came from Laos, and they fled the country during the Vietnam War around 1979. Our family left Laos to seek safety and ended up in a refugee camp in Thailand. Laos is where our roots are, and it shaped much of our early history before we moved to the United States.

explicit_text: I came to the United States as a refugee in 2004 with my family. We first arrived in Wisconsin, where our sponsor helped us for many years, and then we moved to Minnesota to live with my uncle. The United States became our new home where I raised my son and worked hard to build a better life.

explicit_text: The International Organization for Migration, or IOM, played a crucial role in our immigration journey. They helped us find a sponsor in the United States who supported us through paperwork and daily needs. Without IOM, it would have been much harder for my family to resettle here in 2004.

explicit_text: We arrived in Wisconsin on November 1, 2004, where our sponsor welcomed us and took a memorable picture at the airport. I only stayed in Wisconsin for three days before moving to Minnesota, but those first moments there were important as we began our new life in America.

explicit_text: After a brief stay in Wisconsin, I moved to Minnesota to live with my uncle Lee, who believed Saint Paul had better education and job opportunities. Minnesota became the place where I raised my son and adjusted to life in the United States. I remember seeing many pumpkins during Halloween, which was new and interesting to me.

explicit_text: I am part of the Hmong people, who faced many challenges during and after the Vietnam War. My family lived in a refugee camp in Thailand because the Thai government did not want us to stay. Even now, I hope my son will remember and cherish our Hmong culture despite growing up more American.

explicit_text: My first memory of Minnesota included seeing many pumpkins at almost every house, which I later learned was part of Halloween celebrations. This was a new and surprising tradition for me when I arrived in 2004. Halloween became one of the cultural experiences that marked my adjustment to life in the United States.
